title: Workbench to Get the Job Done
summary: A no-frills 2x4 shop workbench with a plywood top and a full lower shelf, built to be sturdy rather than fancy.
description:
This bench earns its name from what it leaves out: no drawers, no vises, no fussy joinery — just a square 2x4 frame carrying a plywood top and a full lower shelf, built to take a beating. Clamping the whole frame together and checking it for square before building the top is the one step worth not skipping, since a top built onto a frame that's slightly out of square will telegraph that error into every straightedge cut you ever make on this bench.

Worth deciding before you cut: if you own heavy stationary tools you'll mount permanently, position one of the under-shelf 2x4s closer to one end rather than centering everything evenly, so it lands under the tool's footprint as a mounting stud. It's a small asymmetry in an otherwise plain frame, and it's the difference between a bench that flexes under a mounted tool and one that doesn't. Countersinking the top screws and skipping the wood filler is a fair call on a bench — this surface is going to take hits regardless.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into 24"-wide, 8 ft strips for both the shelf and the top.

materials.name: Wood filler
materials.note: Optional — a workbench top takes enough abuse that filling screw holes is skippable.

cutList.note: Approximate — measure your own 2x4 stock, since actual dimensions vary by supplier.
Space evenly, except position one off-center as a mounting stud if you plan to bolt down a stationary tool.
Cut the shelf and tabletop pieces from these ripped strips.

steps.title: Assemble the legs
steps.body: Assemble the four legs with the lower shelf stretchers and corner feet blocks. Measure your own 2x4 stock before cutting — actual dimensions vary by region and supplier.

steps.title: Build the shelf frame and cut the shelf
steps.body: Assemble the shelf frame from the long stretchers, then cut the plywood shelf panel to fit. Measure once, then measure again before cutting.

steps.title: Build and check the top frame
steps.body: Before building the top frame, clamp everything together and check width, length, and vertical measurements for square. If you plan to mount heavy stationary tools, position one under-top support closer to one end rather than centering all of them evenly — that support then doubles as a mounting stud for the tool.

steps.title: Assemble the top
steps.body: Screw the plywood top panel down with 1-1/2" construction screws, countersinking the heads so nothing catches your hand while working. Filling the countersunk holes with wood filler is optional on a bench top that's going to take hits regardless.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand any rough edges smooth. A shop bench doesn't need a fussy finish — a coat of something to seal the plywood edges is enough.

images.alt: Simple 2x4 shop workbench with a lower plywood shelf
